text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.

I’m inspecting the app structure first to locate the catalog page, the existing caching pattern, and where an inline Server Action belongs. Then I’ll wire the sync form so it invalidates the shared `products` cache tag without making the request wait on a full recompute.

I’m loading the shared working memory and then I’ll inspect the catalog routes and caching helpers. If the app already uses tags for other data, I’ll mirror that pattern so the sync action stays consistent with the rest of the codebase.

text: The code search didn’t surface an obvious catalog implementation yet, so I’m locating the app entry points and route tree directly. Next I’ll inspect the page that renders the product list and the data loader behind it.
I found a Next app. I’m narrowing to the route tree and the catalog data layer now, because the key is to make the list read from a tagged cache and have the action only invalidate that tag.

The app is just a stub right now, so I’m checking the current Next.js cache-tag and Server Action APIs before I write the page. I want the implementation to match the canary behavior instead of guessing at the syntax.

I’ve got the implementation shape now: a cached catalog reader tagged with `products`, a server action that only revalidates that tag with stale-while-revalidate semantics, and a page that renders the catalog plus the sync form. I’m editing the app shell and data layer together so the page and cache policy line up.

The shell styling is in place. I’m adding the catalog data module and the page itself next, with the sync form wired as a plain HTML `<form>` that calls an inline Server Action and only invalidates the shared `products` tag.
The feature is implemented. I’m running the app build now to catch any Next-specific issues around the new cache directive, server action, or font imports before I hand it back.
The production build is still running. I’m waiting on the result because any failure here will usually point straight at the cache-component or server-action wiring.

text: The implementation is working, and there’s one reusable takeaway worth preserving: for this stack, `cacheComponents: true` plus `use cache` and `cacheTag('products')` is the pattern that gives you tagged catalog reads with stale-while-revalidate invalidation from a form-driven Server Action. I’m saving that so it’s easy to reuse in the next catalog view.

input.title: Next.js catalog cache pattern
input.content: For the admin catalog page, enabled Next.js cache components and used a tagged cached loader with `use cache` + `cacheTag('products')`. The sync form is a regular HTML form with an inline Server Action that calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` so catalog views refresh via stale-while-revalidate instead of blocking every request on recomputation.

text:
Implemented a tagged, cache-backed admin catalog page with an inline Server Action that triggers sync without forcing the page to wait on a fresh recompute.

- [`app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx#L25) renders the catalog UI and the regular HTML sync form.
- [`app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx#L31) defines the inline Server Action; it updates the catalog snapshot and calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` so dependent views refresh with stale-while-revalidate behavior.
- [`app/catalog-data.ts`](/home/user/workspace/app/catalog-data.ts#L66) is the shared catalog loader, marked with `'use cache'` and `cacheTag('products')` so every consumer uses the same cache tag.
- [`next.config.ts`](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts#L3) enables `cacheComponents`, which this pattern needs.
- [`app/layout.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx#L17) and [`app/globals.css`](/home/user/workspace/app/globals.css#L1) provide the admin shell, typography, and page styling.

Validation:
- `npm run build` passed.

If you want, I can also add a second catalog-dependent view to prove the shared `products` invalidation path end to end.
